subject: Mathematics
topic: Plan, Build, Grow — Day 1: Math Stream Worksheet

teacher_guide.big_idea: Math-stream students are seeing the project as a measurement and estimation problem from day one. The space is their first data set — their job today is to look at it like a mathematician and make their first estimates.
learning_goals: Identify length, width, and height as the three key dimensions of a planter box.
Make a reasonable first estimate of the space using pacing or visual comparison.
Identify what information they will need to calculate area and volume.
materials: This worksheet — one per Math-stream student
Ruler or tape measure (optional — for pacing reference)

timing.activity: Students work through this worksheet independently or with EA support. Teacher circulates.

teacher_notes: Read the anchor aloud before students begin. Sketch L/W/H on the board.
Q2 (sketch + estimate) is the core task. Accept rough sketches — the math goal is labeling, not drawing.
Q3 estimates do not need to be accurate. The goal is activating prior knowledge about reasonable sizes.
Students finishing early can calculate: if 1 step ≈ 70 cm, what is their estimated area in cm²?

anchor: Length (L) — the longest side, measured horizontally
Width (W) — the shorter side, measured horizontally
Height (H) — how tall it is, measured vertically
Area = L × W (how much flat surface)
Volume = L × W × H (how much space inside)
worksheet.tip: Estimates are guesses based on what you can see. There is no wrong answer — just show your thinking.

questions.q_text:
Label the three dimensions on this box:

[  box diagram  ]

→ Draw an arrow pointing to the longest side. Label it: L (Length)
→ Draw an arrow pointing to the shorter side. Label it: W (Width)
→ Draw an arrow pointing upward. Label it: H (Height)

Sketch the space where the planter will go (top view — like a map looking down).

[  drawing space  ]

Label: a wall or edge   which side gets sun   where the planter will sit

My sketch shows the planter in the _______________ corner/side of the space.

Estimate the size of the planter box using your steps.

I walked the space: about _______ steps wide,  about _______ steps long.
1 step ≈ 70 cm

Estimated width:  _______ × 70 = _______ cm
Estimated length: _______ × 70 = _______ cm

Does this seem like a reasonable planter size?  Y / N
Why? ___________________

What THREE measurements will our team need to calculate how much soil to buy?

1. ___________________
2. ___________________
3. ___________________

Circle the formula we will use for soil volume:   A = L × W     V = L × W × H     P = 2L + 2W

Before we know the real dimensions — make a prediction:

I think the planter will be about _______ cm long and _______ cm wide.
That would give an area of roughly _______ × _______ = _______ cm².

Circle your role on the math team:   MEASURE   CALCULATE   RECORD DATA   MAKE THE GRAPH

self_check: I labeled length, width, and height on the diagram.
I made at least one estimate using steps or visual comparison.
I know what measurements we need to collect next session.

exit_ticket.title: Day 1 Exit Check — Math Stream
exit_ticket.prompt:
What are the three dimensions of a planter box? Write or say them with their abbreviations.

1. ___ (___)
2. ___ (___)
3. ___ (___)

Which formula gives us the volume?   A = L × W     V = L × W × H
success_criteria: Student named all three dimensions (length, width, height).
Student selected the correct volume formula.
